coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescence strategy based on dual-signal response of carbon dots and o-phenylenediamine for ATP detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Zhihao
Chen, Ruchun
Pan, Shuang
Liu, Hui
Hu, Xiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a convenient method is developed for ratiometric fluorescence detection of ATP (adenosine triphosphate) based on CDs-OPD (carbon dots- o-phenylenediamine). In the absence of ATP, oxOPD, the colored oxidation product of OPD fabricated by a redox reaction occurred between OPD and Cu2+, not only quenches the fluorescence of CDs, but also emits a yellow fluorescence which acts as another signal element. However, ATP will compete with OPD to chelate Cu2+ inhibiting the redox reaction, which hampers the formation of oxOPD resulting in the diminution of IFE (inner filter effect) between CDs and oxOPD, then the fluorescence of CDs restores. ATP-induced dual-signal response of the CDs-OPD/Cu2+ system makes ratiometric assay possible. The method exhibits high sensitivity toward ATP with a detection limit of 0.43 μmol L−1. Furthermore, the semi-quantitative measurement of ATP is available by naked eyes through the color of fluorescence and solution. Finally, the developed method is employed for the quantification of ATP in practical samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
o-Phenylenediamine
Ratiometric fluorescence
Adenosine triphosphate
IFE

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedures for ratiometric detection of ATP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs (Inset: the distribution of CDs size). (B) XRD pattern of CDs. (C) FTIR spectra of CDs. (D) Emission spectra of CDs under different wavelengths of excitation. (E) UV–vis, maximum excitation and emission spectra of CDs.

(A) The solution in the (a) presence and in the (b) absence of ATP under 365 nm UV light and ambient light. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in different conditions. (C) The UV–vis absorption spectra (dash line) of OPD in different conditions, and the emission and excitation spectra (solid line) of CDs. (D) XPS spectra of Cu 2p for Cu2+, Cu2+ + OPD, and Cu2+ + OPD + ATP. (E) The fluorescence lifetime spectra of CDs and CDs + oxOPD. (F) F 440/F 565 of CDs-OPD system in the presence of various metal ions (133 μmol L−1).
The effect of different parameters on the detection of ATP: (A) The reaction time, (B) the pH of solution, (C) the concentration of Cu2+. Conditions: ATP 100 μmol L−1, 100 μL of 10 mmol L−1 OPD.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs-OPD-Cu2+ system after adding different concentrations of ATP (0, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0, 40.0, 60.0, 80.0, 100.0, 150.0, 200.0, 400.0 μmol L−1). (B) The plot of F 440/F 565 against ATP concentration from 0 to 400.0 μmol L−1. (C) The photo of solution after adding different concentrations of ATP under 365 nm UV light and ambient light. (D) Response of the CDs-OPD-Cu2+ system toward various interferences.
The detection mechanism of ATP based on CDs-OPD/Cu2+ system.
Detection of ATP in adenosine disodium triphosphate tablets (n = 5).
Detection of ATP in MCF-7 cell lysates (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescence strategy based on dual-signal response of carbon dots and o-phenylenediamine for ATP detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a convenient method is developed for ratiometric fluorescence detection of ATP (adenosine triphosphate) based on CDs-OPD (carbon dots- o-phenylenediamine). In the absence of ATP, oxOPD, the colored oxidation product of OPD fabricated by a redox reaction occurred between OPD and Cu2+, not only quenches the fluorescence of CDs, but also emits a yellow fluorescence which acts as another signal element. However, ATP will compete with OPD to chelate Cu2+ inhibiting the redox reaction, which hampers the formation of oxOPD resulting in the diminution of IFE (inner filter effect) between CDs and oxOPD, then the fluorescence of CDs restores. ATP-induced dual-signal response of the CDs-OPD/Cu2+ system makes ratiometric assay possible. The method exhibits high sensitivity toward ATP with a detection limit of 0.43 μmol L−1. Furthermore, the semi-quantitative measurement of ATP is available by naked eyes through the color of fluorescence and solution. Finally, the developed method is employed for the quantification of ATP in practical samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: CDs (carbon dots) are a class of carbon-based fluorescence material possessing unique advantages, such as anti-photobleaching, simple synthesis, good water dispersibility, tunable emission, and biocompatibility [1–4]. As a type of heavy-metal-free fluorescence material, environmentally friendly CDs have promising applications in the fields of drug delivery, cell imaging, photodynamic therapy, and nanomedicine [5–9]. CDs with high quantum yield can be synthesized by inexpensive precursors via the facile hydrothermal method, which allows CDs to be ideal fluorescence probes used in various detections. To date, CDs have been utilized as fluorescence probes in the detections of pH, biomolecules, ions, temperature, and enzyme activity due to its excellent photoluminescence properties and functional groups anchored on the surface [10–14].

ATP (adenosine triphosphate) containing two high-energy phosphate bonds is an indispensable energy complex for maintaining many biological processes such as triphosphoric acid cycle, neurotransmission, and DNA synthesis [15–17]. Monitoring ATP concentration is of great importance because it is associated with cellular activity. Up to now, many researches with various approaches for ATP detection including colorimetry [18,19], electrochemical [20,21], photoelectrochemical [22], liquid chromatography [23], and fluorescent sensors [24] have been reported. Nevertheless, sophisticated molecular design and aptamers-based probes make them inconvenient, expensive and time consuming. CDs-based fluorescence method will be an ideal candidate for ATP detection because of simple configuration and rapid response. However, single-signal based fluorescence methods suffer deviations from apparatus efficiency and environmental conditions. Ratiometric fluorescence has drawn great attention in recent years due to its self-calibrated property which excludes the fluctuation of incident light and interference from the environment [25–27]. So far, CDs-based ratiometric methods are established for ATP detection using calcein or quercetin as a medium [28,29]. In addition, ODP (o-phenylenediamine) has been reported in ratiometric fluorescence strategies serving as a fluorescence element and fluorescence quencher after oxidizing into oxOPD [11,30,31]. Thus, the signal change of a ratiometric fluorescence system depends on the amount of oxOPD, which can be controlled by adjusting the activity of oxidizing substances in the system.

Inspired by the fact that certain metal ions such as Cu2+ and Ag+ can oxidize OPD [32]; however, the coordination between metal ions and analyte can inhibit the redox reaction [33]. Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescence method is reported for ATP assay with Cu2+-triggered and ATP-inhibited oxidation of OPD (Scheme 1 ). In the absence of ATP, Cu2+ oxidizes OPD to oxOPD, which obtains an organ emission originated from oxOPD; meanwhile, the fluorescence of CDs is quenched by oxOPD due to IFE (inner filter effect). In the case of adding ATP into the analytical system, the coordination between ATP and Cu2+ inhibits the redox reaction, which keeps the system untouched. The concentration of ATP can be quantitatively determined by the fluorescence intensities of CDs and oxOPD that are directly related to the concentration of free Cu2+. Furthermore, the proposed method is successfully applied in ATP detection in real samples.

OPD, ATP, N-ethylmaleimide, and tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, CO., Ltd., China). Citric acid and copper sulfate pentahydrate were obtained from Oriental Reagent Factory (Chongqing, China). Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments (18.2 MΩ cm). All reagents used above were analytical grade without further purification.

A Rigaku D/MAX 2550 was supplied for X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of CDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CDs was provided by a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN (FEI Company, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired using a Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared spectrum was carried out by an FTIR-8400S. Emission spectra of CDs were obtained on an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence decay traces of CDs with incident light at 370 nm were measured by a FL-TCSPC Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France). Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi was used for the collection of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy.

The procedure of preparing CDs was according to previous work [3]. Briefly, a 20.0 mL of solution containing 0.42 g of OPD and 0.92 g of citric acid was transferred into a Teflon-line autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. Large particles were removed by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 30 min. The CDs stock solution (1.0 mg mL−1) with blue fluorescence was obtained by re-dispersing CDs powder collected by dialyzing supernatant with a 1000 Da dialysis bag and lyophilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for ratiometric detection of ATP
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 50.0 µL of CDs, 100.0 µL of OPD (10 mmol L−1), 200.0 µL of Cu2+ (1 mmol L−1), 1.00 mL of Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 10 mmol L−1), and 150.0 µL of ATP with various concentrations were mixed. The mixtures were kept in a water bath at 60 °C for 60 min and incubated at room temperature for 4 min before fluorescence measurements with 370 nm excitation.

CDs were synthesized by citric acid and OPD via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. The TEM image showed that the average size of CDs was 3.63 nm (Fig. 1 A). Fig. 2 B illustrated the XRD pattern of CDs was a typical disordered graphitic structure with a typical peak at around 24.3° [34]. Fig. 1C depicted the results of functional groups on CDs surface studied by the FTIR spectrum. Stretching vibrations of CH, OH, and NH were assigned to broad bands from 3408 to 2632 cm−1, respectively. A peak at 1708 cm−1 was the stretching vibration of the CO. NH and CN bending vibration could be observed at 1578 and 1387 cm−1. A characteristic peak at 1219 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CO [35,36]. These results elucidated hydrophilic groups including NH2, OH, and COOH anchored on the surface of CDs, which endowed excellent water solubility and dispersion ability for CDs. Synthesized CDs with a constant emission peak position under different excitation displayed excitation-independent emission behavior, indicating the fluorescence originated from surface defects and homogeneous size of CDs [37] (Fig. 1D). The maximum intensity of fluorescence at 440 nm was achieved by excitation at 370 nm. In addition, the absorption peak positioned at 370 nm was consistent with the optimal excitation of CDs (Fig. 1E).

In this work, we reported a method for ratiometric fluorescence detection of ATP based on CDs and oxOPD. oxOPD was produced by the redox reaction of OPD and Cu2+. As displayed in Fig. 2A, a light-yellow color solution with bright blue fluorescence was obtained in the (a) presence of ATP while it displayed as an orange one with yellow fluorescence in the (b) absence of ATP. Previous works have reported that OPD could be oxidized by CoOOH, Cu2+, and MnO2 into oxOPD with yellow fluorescence; at the same time, oxOPD with an absorption peak at 420 nm quenched the fluorescence around this wavelength [11,19,30]. Fig. 2B demonstrated that ATP, Cu2+, and OPD slightly affected the fluorescence of CDs. However, the emission of CDs was quenched sharply in the coexistence of OPD and Cu2+; meanwhile, a new emission at 565 nm appeared. After adding ATP, the fluorescence of CDs recovered and the new peak at 565 nm diminished. The redox reaction occurred when amino groups contained in OPD chelated with Cu2+ [33,38]. ATP added in the system competed with OPD to coordinate with Cu2+ resulting in the restriction of the reaction between OPD and Cu2+ [39–41]. Furthermore, the UV–vis absorption spectra of OPD + Cu2+ in the absence and presence of ATP elucidated that ATP hampered the process of OPD oxidized to oxOPD (Fig. 2C). To further study the role of ATP and the redox reaction between Cu2+ and OPD, XPS measurement was carried out to analyze the oxidation states of Cu2+. The results of Cu2+, Cu2+ + OPD, and Cu2+ + OPD + ATP were illustrated in Fig. 2D, the position around 935 eV was assigned to the Cu 2p3/2 of Cu2+ [42]. The binding energy of Cu 2p3/2 diminished to 932.4 eV after the introduction of OPD, which revealed the existence of Cu+ and Cu0 [43,44], and indicated the redox reaction occurred between Cu2+ and OPD to produce oxOPD. However, for Cu2+ + OPD + ATP system, the binding energy of Cu 2p3/2 was quite similar to that of Cu2+, which proved that ATP could coordinate with Cu2+ and hamper the redox reaction. Thus, the proposed method for ATP detection was feasible according to the ATP-adjusted dual-signal response of the CDs-OPD/Cu2+ system.

To verify the mechanism of CDs fluorescence quenched by oxOPD, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs, and the UV–vis absorption spectra of OPD + Cu2+ were investigated (Fig. 2C). The absorption spectra of oxOPD overlapped with the emission and excitation spectra of CDs, which speculated that IFE or FRET (fluorescence resonance energy transfer) was the mechanism for fluorescence quenching, and the influence both of them were determined by the concentration of ATP which controlled the absorption intensity of the resulted oxOPD. A study of fluorescence lifetime as a valid method was carried out to distinguish IFE and FRET (Fig. 2E). The fluorescence lifetime of CDs + oxOPD was 6.01 ns which was similar to that of CDs (6.31 ns), demonstrating the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs was IFE. To investigate the effect of metal ions, various metal ions were added into the CDs-OPD system in Tris-HCl buffer at pH 7.4. As shown in Fig. 2F, except for Cu2+, other metal ions did not have a significant influence on F 440/F 565 at the same concentration as Cu2+. Notably, Cl- and neutral pH (Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.4) masked Ag+ and Fe3+ possessing strong oxidative ability and prevented the oxidative reaction of OPD triggered by them [45,46].

To achieve the best sensitivity for ATP detection, the effects of reaction time, pH, and the concentration of Cu2+ were studied. Fig. 3 A clearly demonstrated that the OPD-Cu2+ system reached equilibrium after 60 min at 60 °C. The response of the system to ATP under different pH was depicted in Fig. 3B. The variation of △F 440/F 565 was negligible from 7.1 to 7.4 while a significant decline was observed after pH over 7.7, which may be the result of Cu2+ hydrolysis formatting hydroxide which inhibited the redox reaction between Cu2+ and OPD. Thus, pH 7.4 was selected for ATP detection. The concentration of Cu2+ determined the range of ATP detection due to Cu2+ acted as an oxidizing agent in the system. Fig. 3C exhibited that △F 440/F 565 was maximum when the concentration was 133 μmol L−1. The redox reaction was insufficient when the volume below 133 μmol L−1 whereas the redox reaction was hardly restricted by a certain concentration of ATP in the presence of excessive Cu2+ resulting in insensitive signal change.

Fig. 4 A showed the opposite changes of F 440 and F 565 with the ATP concentration increased from 0 to 400.0 μmol L−1. A good linear relationship (F 440/F 565 = 1.7159 + 0.0624C, R 2 = 0.990) of ATP concentration against F 440/ F 565 was found in the range of 1.0 to 100.0 μmol L−1 with the detection limit of 0.43 μmol L−1 (Fig. 4B). Fig. 4C illustrated the fluorescence and color of the solution in the presence of various concentrations of ATP. An orange solution with yellow emission changed to a light yellow one with blue emission as the increase of ATP concentration, indicating that the semi-quantitative measurement of ATP was available by naked eyes through the color of fluorescence and solution. Table 1 listed different methods for APT detection. Compared with previous works for ATP sensing, the proposed method possessed a broad linear range and relatively low detection limit.

Considering the complexity of real samples, there was no doubt that the estimation of the probe selectivity was critical for the determination of ATP. To investigate the selectivity of the system in bio samples, we introduced and studied the effects of adenosine monophosphate (AMP), adenosine diphosphate (ADP), pyrophosphate (PPi), phosphate (Pi), CO3 2−, Cl-, I-, S2O3 2-, fructose, glucose, alanine (Ala), leucine (Leu), histidine (His), glutamic acid (Glu), aspartic acid (Asp), arginine (Arg), glutathione (GSH), homocysteine (Hcy), cysteine (Cys). N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) was added to mask and eliminate the interference from reducing biomass (such as GSH, Hcy and Cys) with sulfhydryl groups [47,48]. The concentration of ATP was 100 μmol L−1, and that of the interference materials were 10-fold of ATP (the concentrations of ADP, AMP, and PPi were 100 μmol L−1; 500.0 μmol L−1 of NEM was introduced as the masking agent). The results in Fig. 4D demonstrated that the variations of F 440/F 565 induced by interference materials were negligible; meanwhile, as the masking agent of GSH, Cys, and Hcy, NEM did not disturb the measurement of ATP. It displayed the high anti-interference and good selectivity of the method.

Adenosine disodium triphosphate trihydrate tablets (20 mg/tablet) and MCF-7 cell lysates were chosen to prove the feasibility of the ratiometric fluorescence probe for ATP detection in real samples. Five tablets were grinded and dissolved with 500.00 mL ultrapure water. After the centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min, the supernatant was used as the real sample. The procedure of the assay was the same as the ATP detection described in the experimental section except replaced the ATP solution with tablets real samples or 50-fold diluted MCF-7 cell lysates (500.0 μmol L−1 of NEM was introduced for eliminating the interference). Analytical results were listed in Table 2 and Table 3 . For tablets samples, the concentration of ATP in the detection was 32.85 μmol L−1 (n = 5, RSD = 3.1%) and the calculated quantality of the original tablet was 19.9 mg which was consistent with the labeled amount. Then a standard titration experiment was established by adding various concentrations of ATP into real samples. The recoveries of ATP were 95.2–103.4% with RSD below 3%. For MCF-7 cell lysates samples, the recoveries were 98.5–104.4% with RSDs of 2.7–3.2%. The results proved that the proposed method had the potential for ATP quantification in the practical application.

In summary, we developed a ratiometric fluorescence method for ATP detection based on the CDs-OPD/Cu2+ system. Initially, Cu2+ oxidized OPD to oxOPD which emitted yellow emission but quenched the blue fluorescence of CDs. However, the coordination between ATP and Cu2+ inhibited the redox reaction of OPD, the fluorescence of CDs recovered and the fluorescence of oxOPD faded, resulting in the color change of fluorescence from yellow to blue. Meanwhile, the color of the solution would change from orange to yellow in different concentrations of ATP. Thus, the semi-quantity of ATP was available through the color of fluorescence or solution. In addition, the mechanism of CDs fluorescence quenching was attributed to IFE, proved by the studies of UV–vis and fluorescence lifetime spectra. The proposed method showed excellent selectivity to ATP then was used to ATP determination in adenosine disodium triphosphate trihydrate tablets and MCF-7 cell lysates with satisfactory results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support for this study by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705132) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2019TY003).
